items.q: Can an endothermic reaction ever be feasible?
items.correct: Yes — if TΔS outweighs ΔH so that ΔG is negative, as in many dissolving and decomposition processes at higher temperatures.
items.misconception: Reactions only happen if they are exothermic.
items.random_wrong: Endothermic reactions are feasible only below 0 °C.
items.why: Students judge feasibility by ΔH alone and forget the entropy term, which can drive endothermic changes like ice melting above 0 °C.

items.q: Why is the second electron affinity of oxygen endothermic?
items.correct: The incoming electron is repelled by the already negative O⁻ ion, so energy must be supplied to force it on.
items.misconception: All electron affinities release energy because atoms 'want' a full outer shell.
items.random_wrong: It is endothermic because oxygen's nucleus is too heavy to attract electrons.
items.why: The 'atoms want full shells' story hides the real physics: adding an electron to an anion must overcome electrostatic repulsion.

items.q: Does a negative ΔG mean the reaction will happen quickly?
items.correct: No — feasibility says nothing about rate; a feasible reaction can be immeasurably slow if its activation energy is high.
items.misconception: A thermodynamically feasible reaction always proceeds at a noticeable rate.
items.random_wrong: A negative ΔG means the reaction must be endothermic.
items.why: Students merge thermodynamics with kinetics; diamond turning to graphite is feasible yet effectively never happens.

items.q: Which change has a positive entropy change?
items.why: Correct answer: Liquid water boiling to steam. “Steam condensing to water” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A reaction is feasible at all temperatures when:
items.correct: ΔH is negative and ΔS is positive
items.misconception: ΔH is positive and ΔS is negative
items.random_wrong: ΔH is negative and ΔS is negative
items.why: It's ΔH is negative and ΔS is positive. “ΔH is positive and ΔS is negative” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The first ionisation energy of magnesium is the enthalpy change for:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mg(g) → Mg⁺(g) + e⁻. “Mg(s) → Mg⁺(g) + e⁻” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The magnitude of lattice enthalpy is greatest when the ions are:
items.why: Correct answer: Small and highly charged. “Large and singly charged” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: For a reaction, ΔH = −100 kJ mol⁻¹ and ΔS = −200 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Calculate the temperature in K at which ΔG = 0.
items.why: The real figure is 500 K. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
